scope: First security pass on path-polymorphism. Branch makes `path` scheme-polymorphic (FilePath + HttpPath), moves consent gate from action handlers into Path verbs, kills IFile/DefaultFileProvider. codeanalyzer v4 NEEDS-WORK doc-only, tester v7 FAIL coverage-only — both quality, not safety. This audit covers the trust boundaries the new HTTP scheme introduces (origin = attacker) plus re-verification of filesystem-permission F1/F2/F4 at HEAD.

attack_surface.exposure: Every FilePath verb (ReadText, ReadBytes, ExistsAsync, Stat, List, WriteText, WriteBytes, Append, Mkdir, Save, Delete, MoveTo, CopyTo, AsBooleanAsync) and every HttpPath verb (same set) calls AuthGate(verb) before any I/O. Cross-scheme base CopyTo/MoveTo delegate to per-side verbs which gate again. BundledTransfer prompts a combined consent only when both source+dest gates require it.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: User consent via output.ask, persisted via Permission.Add. In-root paths auto-grant via IsInRoot (App.AbsolutePath + App.OsAbsolutePath + App.OsDirectory).
mitigations: AuthGate is the single shared helper; impossible to add a verb without seeing the pattern
PerformTransfer (the byte-level work) is private and only reachable through BundledTransfer which gates first
IsInRoot uses path-separator-anchored StartsWith — prefix collisions like /home/userX vs /home/user prevented
Authorize uses a loop not recursion, so adversarial channel input cannot blow the async state machine
gaps: None found in the migration itself.

attack_surface.exposure: Per-App ConcurrentDictionary<string, Func<string, Context, Path>>. Register/From are public C# on the per-App instance. Built-ins (file/http/https) registered in App ctor before any user code. Unknown schemes throw SchemeNotRegistered which Conversion maps to Data.Fail.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: PLang programs cannot reach Scheme.Register today — there is no module action exposing it. App ctor is C# infrastructure.
mitigations: ParseScheme enforces RFC 3986 scheme charset (alphanumeric + +/-/.) — non-conforming strings fall back to file (bare path)
ConcurrentDictionary atomic Register; ordering of built-in registrations preserved (file before user surface)
gaps: Design-note for the future: Scheme.Register overwrites on duplicate key silently. When code.load (out of branch scope, Stage 4 attribute is the seam) lands, a malicious assembly with [PathScheme("file")] will silently MITM the file scheme across the process. Re-registration of a built-in should require explicit consent or hard-error.

attack_surface.exposure: HttpClient(SocketsHttpHandler{AllowAutoRedirect=true}, Timeout=100s), shared process-wide static. Every verb routes through Send/SendAsync which calls SignRequest first. Authorize gate runs on the original URL (HttpPath.Absolute); the dispatched request can follow up to 50 redirects to anywhere.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: The remote origin — this is the branch's new attacker.
mitigations: Per-request HttpRequestMessage; HttpClient handler is stateless
Non-2xx responses become Data.Fail with the status preserved — `let the server respond`
gaps: S1: AllowAutoRedirect=true with no host/IP filter and no per-hop re-authorize. Single redirect from a consented public host to 169.254.169.254 / 127.0.0.1 / RFC1918 reaches IMDS/internal services and returns the body to PLang. High severity.

S2: X-Signature is a custom header — .NET does not strip it on cross-origin redirect (only Authorization). Pairs with S1 and also leaks the PLang identity signature to any directly-fetched origin. Signing envelope is not destination-scoped, so a server downstream that trusts it has no way to verify it was issued for THIS origin.

S3: HttpPath.Absolute canonicalisation includes the query string; on `a` the URL is persisted verbatim into sqlite — query-string secrets (tokens, API keys) become disk-resident with no user warning at consent time.

attack_surface.exposure: ValidatePath resolves relative/PlangRooted/OsRooted paths to absolute OS paths. IsInRoot does StartsWith-with-trailing-sep check against AbsolutePath/OsDirectory/OsAbsolutePath.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: The PLang program may pass arbitrary path strings; Authorize is the gate.
mitigations: Out-of-root paths are returned as-is from ValidatePath — Authorize sees the unmodified Absolute and prompts the user
GetFullPath normalises `..` segments, so `<root>/file:/../../etc/passwd` lands outside root and triggers consent (visible to user, denyable)
On Linux, OS-rooted paths require leading `//` — single-leading-/ stays PlangRooted and re-anchors under app root

findings.vector: HttpClient(SocketsHttpHandler{AllowAutoRedirect=true}) at PLang/app/types/path/http/this.cs:42. Authorize prompts the user with the original URL (HttpPath.Absolute). On grant, _client.SendAsync follows up to 50 redirects (.NET default) silently. No host check, no scheme check, no private/loopback/link-local IP filter, no per-hop re-authorize. A consented request to a public host whose origin returns 302 Location: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/... reaches cloud IMDS; the response body is returned to PLang as data.@this.Ok.
findings.preconditions: User consents to ANY https URL whose origin is attacker-controlled OR compromisable. No further conditions — the response controls the redirect target.
findings.impact: Full SSRF: read cloud-IMDS credentials (AWS/GCP/Azure/Alibaba), reach loopback admin UIs, reach RFC1918 internal services, scan internal network from the PLang host. Blast radius is whatever the redirect target returns + whatever the PLang program does with the body.
findings.exploit_sketch: 1. Stand up evil.example.com responding 302 Location: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role>. 2. Send phishing PLang goal that reads %url% where url is provided by user with a hint of legitimacy (URL shortener / vendor proxy). 3. User answers `y` to `Allow X to read https://evil.example.com/data?`. 4. HttpPath.ReadText: GET evil.example.com -> 302 -> GET IMDS -> body returned. 5. Program forwards body via http.post or stores via file.save — credentials exfiltrated.
findings.proposed_fix: AllowAutoRedirect=false. In Send, on 3xx parse Location, construct fresh HttpPath via Scheme.From, call AuthGate(verb) on the new path, recurse with a hop cap (5-10). Each hop is a fresh consent prompt the user can deny. Composes with the existing consent model.

findings.vector: SignRequest at PLang/app/types/path/http/this.cs:308-334 adds X-Signature header before dispatch. Signing envelope covers {url, method, body} of the ORIGINAL request. The header is a custom name; .NET's HttpClient only strips standard auth headers (Authorization) on redirect, so X-Signature is forwarded on every redirect hop and is also sent to every direct origin a PLang program reaches. Identity is not destination-scoped — the signed envelope does not commit to a host the server can cross-check.
findings.preconditions: Any HttpPath request to any origin discloses the actor's identity envelope; combined with S1 the attacker can choose arbitrary (URL, body, method) tuples to sign by 302-ing from one consented endpoint.
findings.impact: Identity disclosure to arbitrary origins. If any PLang-aware server downstream trusts the signed envelope to authenticate the bearer (filesystem-permission F1 = signer authority not pinned on the verify side; same shape applies here on the issuing side), this is replay material. Even without that, it enables actor fingerprinting and cross-app tracking.
findings.exploit_sketch: 1. Lure consent to https://attacker.tld/. 2. Attacker logs X-Signature. 3. Either: (a) replay against a PLang-aware service that does not pin host in the verify envelope (filesystem-permission F1), or (b) build an identity-fingerprint database across actors.
findings.proposed_fix: Two-layer: (i) drop X-Signature when the request is being sent to a different scheme+host+port than the one Authorize approved (relevant on redirect); (ii) make the signing envelope destination-aware — include host (or canonical URL) inside the signed payload and have signing.verify cross-check on the wire side. Cleanly closes filesystem-permission F1 at the same time.

findings.vector: HttpPath.Absolute (PLang/app/types/path/http/this.cs:85-121) canonicalises the URL including the sorted query string. BuildRequest (this.Authorize.cs:84-88) uses Absolute as the Permission.Path key. On `a` the grant is persisted to sqlite with this key as-is. The consent prompt shows the URL but does not warn that query-string contents will be persisted to disk.
findings.preconditions: User answers `a` to an HttpPath prompt whose URL carries credentials/tokens in the query string.
findings.impact: Tokens / API keys / session IDs end up in <settings>/permission as clear text, surviving `new App()`. User intent (consent to a one-off action) is heavier than user signal (no persistence warning).
findings.exploit_sketch: Not an exploit per se — this is informed-consent failure. Worst case: user pastes a tokenized URL, answers `a`, token is now in the user's local sqlite forever.
findings.proposed_fix: Either: (a) the prompt text for non-file schemes mentions `(this URL including query string will be saved to your local settings)`, or (b) on persist for http schemes, replace query values with placeholders and use Match.Glob so any value matches the path under that host — opt-in.

findings.vector: Re-verified at HEAD: Actor.Permission.VerifySignature (PLang/app/actor/permission/this.cs:146-152) calls signing.verify and trusts result.Success. Ed25519 verify checks signature integrity against the Identity public key embedded INSIDE the signature envelope; it does not constrain whose key signed.
findings.preconditions: An attacker must land a forged-but-self-consistently-signed row in the per-actor sqlite 'permission' table. Today the only writers are Permission.Add/Revoke (user-consent-gated).
findings.impact: Full consent-gate bypass for any path/verb if a non-disk write path to 'permission' ever appears. Ratchet to Critical at that point.
findings.proposed_fix: Pin signer: after signing.verify, assert RawSignature.Identity equals actor's trusted identity public key.
findings.carried_from: filesystem-permission v1/v2 F1 — unchanged by path-polymorphism rename/refactor.

findings.vector: PLang/app/actor/permission/this.cs:127 `if (grantData.RawSignature == null) return true;` shortcuts verification. Comment says 'in-memory unsigned grant', but TryCover is called from both the in-memory and persisted loops; safety depends on Add (line 82) routing unsigned -> in-memory only.
findings.preconditions: An unsigned row in the sqlite 'permission' table (refactor drift, migration import, manual edit).
findings.impact: Auto-trust of an unsigned forged grant. Consent bypass without crypto needed.
findings.proposed_fix: Split TryCover by provenance (in-memory may trust unsigned; persisted must deny null-signature rows).

findings.vector: PLang/app/types/path/permission/this.cs:54-90 — GlobMatches and RegexMatches use Regex.IsMatch with no MatchTimeout. BuildRequest writes Match.Exact today; the latent risk is any future writer (or hand-crafted row) storing a pathological Match.Regex/Glob pattern.
findings.preconditions: A grant row with Match=Regex/Glob and a pathological pattern reaches Find/TryCover.
findings.impact: ReDoS hangs Authorize and therefore every gated I/O.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a Regex MatchTimeout (e.g. 100ms) and treat timeout as no-match.

observations.note: Pre-existing vulnerable NuGet packages surface as NU1902/NU1903 on `dotnet test`: SixLabors.ImageSharp 2.0.0 (incl. one high CVE), HtmlSanitizer 9.0.884, MimeKit 4.10.0. Pre-dates this branch — flagged for a separate dependency-hygiene pass.

SignRequest exception filter at this.cs:330 swallows OperationCanceledException (only NullReferenceException/OOM/StackOverflow are re-thrown). Cancellation during signing then lets the request fly unsigned. Tiny; tighten or document.

Out-of-scope but flagged: when code.load lands (Stage 4 [PathScheme] attribute is the seam), a malicious assembly with [PathScheme("file")] silently MITMs the file scheme because Scheme.Register overwrites on duplicate key. Design code.load to require explicit consent for re-registering a built-in.
